title: AMD's Gigahertz Equivalency: Confused Customers Accept Bad Science
author: Aberdeen Group
abstract: Critical analysis of AMD's Athlon XP gigahertz equivalency model numbering methodology. Examines flaws in AMD's benchmark methodology including non-disclosure of BAPCo SYSmark 2001 results, selective incorporation of Microsoft Media Player bug fixes, inconsistent results between AMD-reported and BAPCo-reported figures, and use of obsolete benchmarks. Identifies four fundamental conceptual flaws: implied fixed relationship over time, assumed specific workload model, mixing of system I/O with processor performance, and platform dependency. Notes AMD's True Performance Initiative had stalled and that European advertisements were reporting GHz-equivalent model numbers as actual GHz ratings.
importance_rationale: Influential early critique of processor marketing benchmarks that contributed to AMD abandoning GHz equivalency; shaped industry discourse on benchmark transparency and benchmarketing practices
relevance_rationale: Benchmark integrity and marketing transparency remain relevant to technology purchasing decisions; specific GHz equivalency metrics are now historical as the industry moved beyond clock speed as a performance indicator
prescience_rationale: Correctly predicted AMD would abandon GHz equivalency methodology (AMD dropped it by 2003); correctly anticipated benchmark-based marketing would face increased scrutiny from trade press and regulators; correctly noted TPI was stalling and would require wholesale model number readjustment
